title: Introduction to libml
text: libml package provides functions create training datasets, perform univariate tests (via calc_univariate(), train classifiers, plot ROC curves. models types supported package primarily geared toward: Naive Bayes Random forest Logistic regression also limited support k-nearest neighbors models. exception “robust” Naive Bayes, models created using associated R packages directly. libml package provides wrappers performing cross validation, making predictions, creating ROC curves. package biased toward binary classification result many functions assume two classes exist. keep track class positive class, tibble called tr_data object defined use package. “Response” column factor class, ordered positive class second label. tr_data data frames can created using create_train() function. ROC curves related functions standard input consisting : truth: vector actual class labels predicted: numeric vector (order truth) class predictions

what: Introduction & Brief Review of ROC Curves
title: Plotting ROC Curves
text: vignette describes plot ROC curves using functions found libml. starting code walk , however, provide quick refresher ROC curves. Skip next section information already familiar . receiver operating characteristic curve, ROC curve, graphical device helps visualize discriminatory ability binary classifier system. produce ROC curve, sensitivity (aka true positive rate) specificity (aka false positive rate) calculated different values continuous set data. translate visualization, ROC curve made plotting sensitivity (y-axis) 1–specificity (x-axis) calculated values. ROC curve follows diagonal (unit) line y = x produces false positive results rate true positive results. Classifiers reasonable accuracy ROC curve highest point upper left corner plot, y = x line. accuracy can quantified using area ROC curve (AUC) metric, measure ability test discriminate groups correctly identify condition present. higher AUC, better model making accurate predictions. example, AUC 0.5 represents test discriminating ability (basically better chance), AUC 1.0 represents test perfect discrimination. common see graphical representations ROC curve paired corresponding numeric AUC value. Fun fact: ROC curve first developed electrical engineers radar engineers World War II, purpose detecting enemy objects battlefields operators military radar receivers. curve got name.

title: Plotting ROC Curves
text: ROC curves frequently used visualization tool model assessment, classifier performance, biomarker evaluation, . Consequently, libml package well-equipped easily quickly generate visualize ROC curves AUC values. basic level, individual ROC curves can generated using geom_roc() function. function creates ggplot2-compatible “geom” layer generates plots ROC curve, utilizing ggplot2-style grammar graphics. primary input geom_roc() output roc_xy() (also libml), generally used support wrapper functions plot_emp_roc() plot_boot_roc(). functions generate ROC curve plots, provide advanced options plot annotation confidence interval generation, respectively. illustrate geom_roc() can used create ROC curve, must first generate example data set work . roc_xy() function create x y values required plot curve data set. Now obtained coordinates ROC curve, can use ggplot() geom_roc() plot curve:  ’ll notice simplistic plot; geom_roc() merely creates ROC curve layer (without modifying axis labels, plot title, etc.), plot pretty basic uses default ggplot2 theme. can class bit applying /modifying additional theme elements:  fitted line can layered top curve using geom_rocfit(). Like geoms, features fit line (like color, size, line type, etc.) can customized:  ’ll notice took decent number lines code get point, even though final product still rather simplistic plot. wrapper functions mentioned previously alleviate issue baking steps one single function.
code: # Generate example data true <- rep(c("control", "disease"), each = 10) pred <- withr::with_seed(8, c(rnorm(10, mean = 0.4, sd = 0.2),                               rnorm(10, mean = 0.6, sd = 0.2)))  # Calculate x & y coords for the ROC curve using data generated above rocxy <- roc_xy(true, pred, "disease") |>   data.frame()     # must cast to a data frame for ggplot() head(rocxy) #>   x   y #> 1 0 0.0 #> 2 0 0.1 #> 3 0 0.2 #> 4 0 0.3 #> 5 0 0.4 #> 6 0 0.5 # Set up ggplot2-style plot mappings ggplot(data = rocxy, mapping = aes(x = x, y = y)) +   geom_roc(color = "blue")    # add curve layer to plot p <- ggplot(data = rocxy, aes(x = x, y = y)) +   labs(x = "1 - Specificity", y = "Sensitivity") +   theme(panel.grid = element_blank()) +   geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, size = 0.2,               linetype = "dashed", color = "grey") +   theme(panel.grid.minor = element_line(linetype = "dashed",                                         color = "grey", size = 0.2),         panel.grid.major = element_line(linetype = "dashed",                                         color = "grey", size = 0.2)) +   geom_roc(color = "blue") #> Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0. #> ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead. #> This warning is displayed once per session. #> Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was generated. #> Warning: The `size` argument of `element_line()` is deprecated as of ggplot2 3.4.0. #> ℹ Please use the `linewidth` argument instead. #> This warning is displayed once per session. #> Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was generated.  p p + geom_rocfit(data = rocxy, color = "blue", lty = "dashed")

title: Plotting ROC Curves
text: ’s often advantageous annotate ROC curves AUC values, draw points along curve, add visual features make plot easier interpret informative. Additionally, comparing multiple ROC curves, ’s useful layer curves top aid visual comparison simplify overall presentation. However, adding annotation /text layers customizing theme elements can become labor intensive messy 2+ curves (plus fit lines, cases) plot. wrapper function plot_emp_roc() streamlines process generating custom annotations hood.  plots generated plot_emp_roc() can also layered, compare ROC curves generated various data sets:  Note: adding plots together, add = argument (integer) must passed set plotting layer (zero indexed) desired curve. used stagger AUC text annotation values overlap.
code: plot_emp_roc(truth     = true,              predicted = pred,              pos_class = "disease",              plot_fit  = "both",  # both ROC and fit line should be drawn              col       = "red",              shape     = 10) # Generate additional datasets n <- 50 data <- lapply(1:4, function(.x) {   withr::with_seed(101,     data.frame(truth = rep(c("control", "disease"), each = n),                pred  = c(rnorm(n), rnorm(n, mean = .x / 2)))   ) }) |> setNames(paste0("df_", 1:4L))  # Create a color palette to distinguish the curves cols <- unlist(libml:::col_palette)  # Create a base plot & layer additional curves layered on top plot_emp_roc(data$df_1$truth, data$df_1$pred, "disease", col = cols[1L]) +   plot_emp_roc(data$df_2$truth, data$df_2$pred, "disease", col = cols[2L], add = 1) +   plot_emp_roc(data$df_3$truth, data$df_3$pred, "disease", col = cols[3L], add = 2) +   plot_emp_roc(data$df_4$truth, data$df_4$pred, "disease", col = cols[4L], add = 3)

text: system/syntax can also used wrapper function, plot_boot_roc(), similar syntax plot_emp_roc() also built around geom_roc(). primary difference two plot_emp_roc() options customizing plot appearance, plot_boot_roc() designed generate visualize bootstrapped CI95 intervals via shaded region addition ROC curve.  plots can also layered, similar syntax plot_emp_roc():
code: # Simulate a new example dataset for this example n <- 50 true <- rep(c("control", "disease"), each = n) pred <- withr::with_seed(1, c(rnorm(n, 0.2, 0.2), rnorm(n, 0.5, 0.2))) plot_boot_roc(true, pred, "disease", shade.color = "blue", color = "red", nboot = 200) #> Warning in geom_roc(data = rocxy, aes(x = x, y = y), ...): Ignoring unknown parameters: #> `shade.colour` plot_boot_roc(data$df_3$truth, data$df_3$pred, "disease", shade.color = cols[4L],               color = cols[4L], nboot = 200) +   plot_boot_roc(data$df_2$truth, data$df_2$pred, "disease", shade.color = cols[3L],                 color = cols[3L], nboot = 200, add = TRUE) +   plot_boot_roc(data$df_1$truth, data$df_1$pred, "disease", shade.color = cols[2L],                 color = cols[2L], nboot = 200, add = TRUE) #> Warning in geom_roc(data = rocxy, aes(x = x, y = y), ...): Ignoring unknown parameters: `shade.colour` #> Ignoring unknown parameters: `shade.colour` #> Ignoring unknown parameters: `shade.colour`

code: # pre-process simulated data data  <- wranglr::simdata feats <- grep("^seq.", names(data), value = TRUE) for ( i in feats ) {   data[[i]] <- log10(data[[i]]) } data  <- center_scale(data) t_tbl <- attr(data, "Col.Meta") |>   dplyr::mutate(feature = libml:::add_seq(SeqId)) |>   select(feature, SeqId, TargetFullName, EntrezGeneSymbol, UniProt)  head(t_tbl) #> # A tibble: 6 × 5 #>   feature     SeqId   TargetFullName           EntrezGeneSymbol UniProt #>   <chr>       <chr>   <chr>                    <chr>            <chr>   #> 1 seq.2802.68 2802-68 Protein-kinase-subunit-1 4HG              NBXVHJ  #> 2 seq.9251.29 9251-29 Protein-kinase-subunit-2 QKG              UL9R2V  #> 3 seq.1942.70 1942-70 Protein-kinase-subunit-3 JHQ8             Z6PS9G  #> 4 seq.5751.80 5751-80 Protein-kinase-subunit-4 NV6Y             OBX5GT  #> 5 seq.9608.12 9608-12 Protein-kinase-subunit-5 IHW              72CM6T  #> 6 seq.3459.49 3459-49 Protein-kinase-subunit-6 JP4B             OKGFQ2

title: Using Nested Tibbles for Univariate Analysis
text: preparation steps place, iterate rows add columns analysis grows, combination dplyr::mutate() purrr::map(): Done! now self-contained tibble object entire t-test analysis. t_stat list-column contains “t-tests” neighboring columns contain corresponding p-values fdr corrections. rows (features) ordered p-value appropriate rank order added.
code: t_tbl <- t_tbl |>   mutate(     t_stat  = map(feature, ~ as.formula(paste(.x, "~ class_response")) |>                   t.test(data = data)),        # fit t-tests     p_value = map_dbl(t_stat, "p.value"),      # pull out p-values     fdr     = p.adjust(p_value, method = "fdr") # FDR test correction   ) |>   arrange(p_value) |>           # Re-order by `p-value`   mutate(rank = row_number())   # add ranks column  # each row represents a feature length(feats) #> [1] 40 nrow(t_tbl) #> [1] 40  # the analysis table t_tbl #> # A tibble: 40 × 9 #>    feature     SeqId   TargetFullName         EntrezGeneSymbol UniProt t_stat  p_value     fdr  rank #>    <chr>       <chr>   <chr>                  <chr>            <chr>   <list>    <dbl>   <dbl> <int> #>  1 seq.1942.70 1942-70 Protein-kinase-subuni… JHQ8             Z6PS9G  <htest> 6.91e-8 2.76e-6     1 #>  2 seq.2802.68 2802-68 Protein-kinase-subuni… 4HG              NBXVHJ  <htest> 1.57e-6 2.95e-5     2 #>  3 seq.9251.29 9251-29 Protein-kinase-subuni… QKG              UL9R2V  <htest> 2.21e-6 2.95e-5     3 #>  4 seq.9608.12 9608-12 Protein-kinase-subuni… IHW              72CM6T  <htest> 1.71e-5 1.71e-4     4 #>  5 seq.5751.80 5751-80 Protein-kinase-subuni… NV6Y             OBX5GT  <htest> 3.63e-4 2.90e-3     5 #>  6 seq.3300.26 3300-26 Protein-kinase-subuni… G6Q              HPTSIG  <htest> 8.41e-2 4.49e-1     6 #>  7 seq.3459.49 3459-49 Protein-kinase-subuni… JP4B             OKGFQ2  <htest> 8.58e-2 4.49e-1     7 #>  8 seq.8142.63 8142-63 Protein-kinase-subuni… XOR              68Z14U  <htest> 9.06e-2 4.49e-1     8 #>  9 seq.1130.49 1130-49 Protein-kinase-subuni… X6K              IKSPO3  <htest> 1.01e-1 4.49e-1     9 #> 10 seq.3896.5  3896-5  Protein-kinase-subuni… BZFW             584QKS  <htest> 1.41e-1 5.00e-1    10 #> # ℹ 30 more rows

Summary method returns summary_confusion_matrix class object (list) consisting : confusion: class counts based confusion matrix. metrics: Performance metric estimates, n, associated binomial 95% confidence intervals. Note MCC range [-1, 1], therefore confidence intervals calculated metric (calc_ci_binom() expects probability value). stats: F-measure, G-mean, Weighted Accuracy.

title: Calculate confusion matrix — calc_confusion
text: Assume 2x2 table notation: : $$TN = True Negative$$ $$TP = True Positive$$ $$FN = False Negative$$ $$FP = False Positive$$ summary calculations : $$Sensitivity = Recall = TP / (TP + FN)$$ $$Specificity = TN / (TN + FP)$$ $$Precision = PPV = TP / (TP + FP)$$ $$NPV = TN / (TN + FN)$$ $$Accuracy = (TP + TN) / (TP + FN + TN + FP)$$ $$Balanced Accuracy = (Sensitivity + Specificity) / 2$$ $$Prevalence = (FN + TP) / (TP + FN + TN + FP)$$ $$Matthew's Correlation Coefficient = TP x TN - FP x FN /                             sqrt( (TP + FP) (TP + FN) (TN + FP) (TN + FN) )$$

title: Calculate ROC Curve Parameters — calc_roc_fit
text: Use non-linear least squares calculate parameters \(\alpha, \beta\) necessary draw ROC curve empirical data. objective function assumed form: $$y \sim 1 - (1 - x^\beta)^{(1/\alpha)}$$ $$ \alpha \(0, 1]$$ $$ \beta \(0, 1]$$ $$ x \(0, 1]$$  extreme x-values (0 1) y = x equilibrium line.

title: Plot a ROC Curve — geom_roc
text: geom_rocfit(): Add fitted line (layer) ROC.

title: Plot a ROC Curve — geom_roc
text: Stu Field, Amanda Hiser

title: Plot a ROC Curve — geom_roc
code: library(ggplot2)  # Generate dummy data true <- rep(c("control", "disease"), each = 10) pred <- withr::with_seed(8,   c(rnorm(10, mean = 0.4, sd = 0.2),     rnorm(10, mean = 0.6, sd = 0.2)) ) rocxy <- roc_xy(true, pred, "disease") |> data.frame()  # Plotting options ggplot(rocxy, aes(x = x, y = y)) + geom_roc()  ggplot(rocxy, aes(x = x, y = y)) + geom_roc(col = "blue")  ggplot(rocxy, aes(x = x, y = y)) + geom_roc(col = "blue", outline = FALSE)   # Draw a fit line with `geom_rocfit()` # (to add a fit-layer, you *must* pass the data argument) ggplot(rocxy, aes(x = x, y = y)) + geom_rocfit(data = rocxy)   # Layer a fit line over a ROC curve ggplot(rocxy, aes(x = x, y = y)) +   geom_roc(col = "blue") +   geom_rocfit(data = rocxy, col = "red", linetype = "dashed")   # Multiple curves can be drawn on the same plot. # First, generate a 2nd set of dummy data true2 <- rep(c("control", "disease"), each = 20) pred2 <- withr::with_seed(9,   c(rnorm(20, mean = 0.4, sd = 0.2),     rnorm(20, mean = 0.8, sd = 0.2)) )  # Cast input to a data frame (this is required for ggplot) rocxy2 <- roc_xy(true2, pred2, "disease") |> data.frame()  # The 2nd line can be added via standard `+` ggplot2 syntax, # but the data argument must be passed for each geom, as each curve was # generated from a unique dataset ggplot() +   geom_roc(aes(x = x, y = y), data = rocxy, col = "red") +   geom_roc(aes(x = x, y = y), data = rocxy2, col = "blue")

title: Mack-Wolfe Test — mack_wolfe
text: Calculates Mack-Wolfe test single vector numeric data. Jonckheere-Terpstra (JT) test Jonckheere trend test special case Mack-Wolfe peak set one ends.

title: Mack-Wolfe Test — mack_wolfe
code: mack_wolfe(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'formula' mack_wolfe(formula, data, ...)  # S3 method for class 'numeric' mack_wolfe(   x,   group,   peak = "jt",   rm_outliers = FALSE,   alpha = 0.05,   nperm = NULL,   ... )

title: Mack-Wolfe Test — mack_wolfe
text: x numeric(n). numeric vector values. empty parameters (see examples), example Hollander & Wolfe computed. ... Additional arguments passed downstream S3 methods. formula formula specifying lhs rhs. data data.frame containing variables formula. group factor(n). vector groupings matched x. factor levels used grouping information. peak character(1). string corresponding desired peak factor level. peak = "jt" (default) JT-test performed. peak = NULL, "peak unknown" version Mack-Wolfe test performed. rm_outliers logical(1). statistical outliers (\(6 * mad\) \(5x\)) removed? See wranglr::remove_outliers(). alpha numeric(1). desired significance level. nperm integer(1). number Monte-Carlo simulations perform. NULL, p-value approximation used (length(x) > 10).

title: Mack-Wolfe Test — mack_wolfe
text: Returns mack_wolfe class object: Ap: Mack-Wolfe test statistic (peak known) Astar: Normal approximation test statistic p_value: two-sided p-value associated Normal approximation (pnorm()) Acrit: critical value z-distribution requested significance level (alpha) alpha: significance level critical value evaluated peak: peak determined test statistic groups: factor levels supplied group parameter.

title: Mack-Wolfe Test — mack_wolfe
text: Myles Hollander Douglas . Wolfe (1973). Nonparametric Statistical Methods. New York: John Wiley & Sons. Pages 115-120, 215.

title: Mack-Wolfe Test — mack_wolfe
text: Stu Field, Michael Mehan

title: Mack-Wolfe Test — mack_wolfe
code: x <- c(36.0, 33.6, 26.9, 35.8, 30.1, 31.2, 35.3,   # g1        39.9, 29.1, 43.4,                           # g2        44.6, 54.4, 48.2, 55.7, 50,                 # g3        53.8, 53.9, 62.5, 46.6,                     # g4        44.3, 34.1, 35.7, 35.6,                     # g5        31.7, 22.1, 30.7)                           # g6 months <- c(g1 = "Jan-Feb",             g2 = "Mar-Apr",             g3 = "May-Jun",             g4 = "Jul-Aug",             g5 = "Sep-Oct",             g6 = "Nov-Dec") group  <- rep(months, c(7, 3, 5, 4, 4, 3)) group  <- factor(group, months) peak   <- "Jul-Aug"  mack_wolfe(x, group, peak) #> ══ Mack-Wolfe test for umbrella alternatives (peak known) ═════════════ #>  #> • Ap       = '157' #> • Ap*      = '4.1848' #> • n        = '26' #> • p_value  = '0.000028539' #> • Acrit    = '1.6449' #> • alpha    = '0.05' #> • groups   = 'Jan-Feb', 'Mar-Apr', 'May-Jun', 'Jul-Aug', 'Sep-Oct', 'Nov-Dec' #> • peak     = 'Jul-Aug | k = 4' #>  #> ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════  # run example from text (same as above) mack_wolfe() #> ══ Mack-Wolfe test for umbrella alternatives (peak known) ═════════════ #>  #> • Ap       = '157' #> • Ap*      = '4.1848' #> • n        = '26' #> • p_value  = '0.000028539' #> • Acrit    = '1.6449' #> • alpha    = '0.05' #> • groups   = 'Jan-Feb', 'Mar-Apr', 'May-Jun', 'Jul-Aug', 'Sep-Oct', 'Nov-Dec' #> • peak     = 'Jul-Aug | k = 4' #>  #> ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════  # use Monte-Carlo permutation to estimate p-value mack_wolfe(NA, nperm = 100L) #> ℹ Performing Monte-Carlo permutation estimate of p-value. #> ══ Mack-Wolfe test for umbrella alternatives (peak known) ═════════════ #>  #> • Ap       = '157' #> • Ap*      = '4.1848' #> • n        = '26' #> • p_value  = '0' #> • Acrit    = '1.6449' #> • alpha    = '0.05' #> • groups   = 'Jan-Feb', 'Mar-Apr', 'May-Jun', 'Jul-Aug', 'Sep-Oct', 'Nov-Dec' #> • peak     = 'Jul-Aug | k = 4' #>  #> ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════  # S3 formula method df <- data.frame(var = x, gr = group) mack_wolfe(var ~ gr, data = df, peak = peak) #> ══ Mack-Wolfe test for umbrella alternatives (peak known) ═════════════ #>  #> • Ap       = '157' #> • Ap*      = '4.1848' #> • n        = '26' #> • p_value  = '0.000028539' #> • Acrit    = '1.6449' #> • alpha    = '0.05' #> • groups   = 'Jan-Feb', 'Mar-Apr', 'May-Jun', 'Jul-Aug', 'Sep-Oct', 'Nov-Dec' #> • peak     = 'Jul-Aug | k = 4' #>  #> ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

title: Plot Empirical ROC Curve — plot_emp_roc
text: truth character(n) factor(n). vector true class names. instances also pass pos_class argument defining positive/event class. predicted numeric(n). numeric vector class probabilities. pos_class character(1). Name "positive" "event" class. auc logical(1). AUC printed plot? add integer(1). position plotting stack indicating add ROC curve relative existing plot. Zero indexing used, thus add = 0L (default) refers new plot add = 1L add ROC layer existing plot correctly position AUC annotations shifted accordingly. boot_auc logical(1). bootstrap confidence intervals AUC calculated? adj numeric(1) [0, 1]. Coordinates used align AUC text. auc_pos numeric(1) [0, 1] indicating place AUC text. Must length == 2L, indicating x-axis y-axis positions, respectively. default, AUC placed slightly right center plot. auc_shift vertical (downward) shift AUC text values multiple ROCs plotted. auc_label character(1). Adds additional label AUC text, .e. "AUC = 0.99", label "Extra Text": "Extra Text AUC = 0.99"). Must added individually plot. auc_size numeric(1). size AUC text. shape numeric(1). Shape points (0 25), similar pch graphics::points(). See ggplot2::geom_point(). size numeric(1). Size points. Similar cex graphics::points(). Modifying size affect plot shape set NULL (default). See [geom_point())]. [geom_point())]: R:geom_point()) cutoff numeric(1). decision cutoff, aka operating point, positive classes. default, operating point set 0.5. Alternatively, choosing negative number calculates cutoff corresponding maximum perpendicular distance curve unit diagonal. CI95 operating point (boxes) can omitted setting cutoff = NA. cutoff_size numeric(1) [0, 1], character size cutoff point symbol. cutoff_shape numeric(1). point symbol used cutoff point plotted ROC. Defaults diamond (23). col character(1) integer(1). Specify colors lines, points, bar, box, ROC. ci95 logical(1). confidence intervals plotted? lwd numeric(1). Line width (see par()). outline logical(1). black outlines drawn around main plot line? boxes logical(1). confidence interval boxes drawn (showing joint CI95 sensitivity specificity confidence intervals) cutoff point? box_alpha numeric(1) [0, 1], shading transparency confidence interval box given cutoff. debug logical(1). debugging mode activated? activated, annotates values plotting steps cutoff, prints "positive class", prints prediction data console, various internal objects useful debugging. plot_fit logical(1). maximum-likelihood (least-squares ML fails) fit curve plotted? plot_fit = TRUE, fit plotted, without empirical ROC. Can also plot_fit = "", fit added empirical ROC. do_grid logical(1). grid lines added ROC?

out-of-bag #>   pred_class prob_setosa prob_virginica #> 1     setosa  0.88823529      0.1117647 #> 2  virginica  0.31188119      0.6881188 #> 3  virginica  0.13372093      0.8662791 #> 4  virginica  0.05405405      0.9459459 #> 5  virginica  0.47120419      0.5287958 #> 6     setosa  0.78974359      0.2102564 calc_predictions(rf, test) #> #
